content.Text:
[Wed 2026-04-22 10:08 PDT] Create a new interactive HTML tool at docs/tournament.html for GraphVisual - a "Graph Tournament Bracket" visualization.

## What to build

A self-contained HTML page (no external dependencies except docs/styles.css) that lets users:

1. **Input a graph** via text (adjacency list format, one edge per line like "A-B") or pick from 7 presets (Petersen, Complete K5, Cycle C8, Star S6, Binary Tree, Grid 3x3, Random)
2. **Run a tournament** where nodes compete head-to-head based on configurable metrics:
   - Degree centrality (default)
   - Closeness centrality 
   - Betweenness centrality (approximate)
   - PageRank
3. **Two tournament modes:**
   - **Single Elimination** - random seeded bracket, losers eliminated
   - **Round Robin** - every node plays every other, ranked by wins
4. **Interactive Canvas bracket visualization:**
   - For elimination: classic bracket tree with match lines, winner highlighted green, loser gray
   - For round robin: standings table + final ranking podium
   - Animated progression (step through rounds with Play/Pause/Step buttons)
5. **Proactive Insights panel** (agentic features):
   - Detect "upsets" (lower-seeded node beating higher-seeded)
   - Identify "dominant champions" vs "narrow winners" based on margin
   - Suggest optimal seeding based on metric distribution
   - Flag nodes that consistently underperform their seed

## Technical requirements

- Use the same dark theme from styles.css (var(--bg), var(--surface), var(--accent), etc.)
- Use the sidebar nav pattern from other pages (see spectrum.html for reference)
- Sidebar links: Home (index.html), Tournament (tournament.html active), Stats (stats.html)
- All computation in vanilla JS, no libraries
- Canvas element for bracket drawing
- Responsive layout
- ~800-1200 lines total

## File to create
- docs/tournament.html

Do NOT modify any other files. Just create the one new HTML file.
I’m creating `docs/tournament.html` and first pulling the existing page structure and theme details from `docs/spectrum.html` and `docs/styles.css` so the new tool matches the current docs layout.
I have the shared theme and nav skeleton. Next I’m checking one interactive doc page for layout conventions and any mobile patterns before I draft the new page in one file.

I have enough context to build this as a single self-contained page. I’m wiring one file with three parts: the docs shell and controls, the graph/tournament engine in vanilla JS, and the visualization layer for bracket, standings, podium, and insights.

GraphVisual

Documentation
 🏠Home 🌈Spectrum 📈Statistics

🌈 Graph Eigenvalue Spectrum Analyzer

The GraphSpectrumAnalyzer computes eigenvalues of the adjacency and Laplacian matrices, providing key spectral graph theory metrics without any external dependencies.

Computed Metrics

MetricDescription
Spectral Radius (λ₁)Largest adjacency eigenvalue — bounds chromatic number, counts walks, measures graph energy contribution.
Spectral Gap (λ₁ − λ₂)Difference between the two largest adjacency eigenvalues. Larger gaps indicate better expansion properties.
Algebraic Connectivity (a)Second smallest Laplacian eigenvalue (Fiedler value). Zero iff the graph is disconnected. Measures connectivity robustness.
Graph EnergySum of absolute adjacency eigenvalues, Σ|λᵢ|. A measure from mathematical chemistry for molecular stability.
Connected ComponentsCount of zero Laplacian eigenvalues (multiplicity of 0 in the Laplacian spectrum).
Spectral Radius Ratio (ρ/√Δ)Spectral radius divided by square root of maximum degree. Indicates deviation from regularity.

Usage

GraphSpectrumAnalyzer analyzer = new GraphSpectrumAnalyzer(graph);
analyzer.compute();

System.out.println(analyzer.getSummary());
System.out.printf("Spectral radius: %.4f%n", analyzer.getSpectralRadius());
System.out.printf("Algebraic connectivity: %.4f%n", analyzer.getAlgebraicConnectivity());
System.out.printf("Graph energy: %.4f%n", analyzer.getGraphEnergy());
System.out.printf("Components: %d%n", analyzer.getComponentCount());

double[] adjEigs = analyzer.getAdjacencyEigenvalues();
double[] lapEigs = analyzer.getLaplacianEigenvalues();

Interpretation Guide

  • High spectral gap → Good expander graph (mixes quickly, robust connectivity)
  • Low algebraic connectivity → Bottleneck in the graph (nearly disconnected components)
  • Spectral radius ≈ average degree → Graph is close to regular
  • High graph energy → Rich edge structure, commonly seen in dense molecular graphs

Algorithm

Uses Householder tridiagonalization followed b...

GraphVisual

Documentation
 🏠Home 🌈Spectrum 📈Statistics

🌈 Graph Eigenvalue Spectrum Analyzer

The GraphSpectrumAnalyzer computes eigenvalues of the adjacency and Laplacian matrices, providing key spectral graph theory metrics without any external dependencies.

Computed Metrics

MetricDescription
Spectral Radius (λ₁)Largest adjacency eigenvalue — bounds chromatic number, counts walks, measures graph energy contribution.
Spectral Gap (λ₁ − λ₂)Difference between the two largest adjacency eigenvalues. Larger gaps indicate better expansion properties.
Algebraic Connectivity (a)Second smallest Laplacian eigenvalue (Fiedler value). Zero iff the graph is disconnected. Measures connectivity robustness.
Graph EnergySum of absolute adjacency eigenvalues, Σ|λᵢ|. A measure from mathematical chemistry for molecular stability.
Connected ComponentsCount of zero Laplacian eigenvalues (multiplicity of 0 in the Laplacian spectrum).
Spectral Radius Ratio (ρ/√Δ)Spectral radius divided by square root of maximum degree. Indicates deviation from regularity.

Usage

GraphSpectrumAnalyzer analyzer = new GraphSpectrumAnalyzer(graph);
analyzer.compute();

System.out.println(analyzer.getSummary());
System.out.printf("Spectral radius: %.4f%n", analyzer.getSpectralRadius());
System.out.printf("Algebraic connectivity: %.4f%n", analyzer.getAlgebraicConnectivity());
System.out.printf("Graph energy: %.4f%n", analyzer.getGraphEnergy());
System.out.printf("Components: %d%n", analyzer.getComponentCount());

double[] adjEigs = analyzer.getAdjacencyEigenvalues();
double[] lapEigs = analyzer.getLaplacianEigenvalues();

Interpretation Guide

  • High spectral gap → Good expander graph (mixes quickly, robust connectivity)
  • Low algebraic connectivity → Bottleneck in the graph (nearly disconnected components)
  • Spectral radius ≈ average degree → Graph is close to regular
  • High graph energy → Rich edge structure, commonly seen in dense molecular graphs

Algorithm

Uses Householder tridiagonalization followed by the implicit QR algorithm with Wilkinson shifts. This is a standard O(n³) approach suitable for graphs up to several hundred vertices. No external linear algebra libraries are required.

Output Example

=== Graph Eigenvalue Spectrum ===
Vertices: 8
Connected components: 1
Spectral radius (λ₁):       2.828427
Spectral gap (λ₁ − λ₂):     1.414214
Algebraic connectivity (a):  0.585786
Graph energy:                11.313708
Spectral radius ratio (ρ/√Δ): 1.632993

Top 10 adjacency eigenvalues:
  λ_1 = 2.828427
  λ_2 = 1.414214
  λ_3 = 0.000000
  ...

Spectral classification: Moderate expansion

📊 GraphVisual
Guide API Cookbook

🎯 Centrality Radar Chart

Compare node centrality profiles with interactive radar/spider charts. Visualize degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality side-by-side for up to 8 nodes.

Overview

The CentralityRadarExporter generates a self-contained HTML page with an interactive radar chart and sortable ranking table. It computes all three centrality metrics using NodeCentralityAnalyzer and presents them in a visual, comparative format.

Features

  • Radar Chart: Spider/radar visualization of degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality
  • Multi-Node Comparison: Select up to 8 nodes with distinct colors
  • Sortable Table: Click column headers to sort by any metric
  • Top-N Filter: Focus on top 5, 10, 20, or all nodes
  • Search: Filter nodes by ID in real-time
  • Dark/Light Theme: Toggle between themes
  • JSON Export: Download selected or all centrality data
  • Summary Stats: At-a-glance most central nodes and averages

Usage

Java API

Graph<String, edge> graph = /* your JUNG graph */;
CentralityRadarExporter exporter = new CentralityRadarExporter(graph);
exporter.setTitle("Student Network Centrality");
exporter.export(new File("centrality-radar.html"));

// Or get HTML as string
String html = exporter.exportToString();

How It Works

  1. Computes degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality for every node
  2. Renders a ranking table sorted by combined score (0.3×degree + 0.4×betweenness + 0.3×closeness)
  3. Click any row to add that node to the radar chart (up to 8 simultaneous)
  4. The radar chart draws each node's three centrality values as a filled polygon
  5. Overlapping polygons reveal complementary strengths — e.g., a node with high betweenness but low degree

Interpreting the Chart

...

📊 GraphVisual
Guide API Cookbook

🎯 Centrality Radar Chart

Compare node centrality profiles with interactive radar/spider charts. Visualize degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality side-by-side for up to 8 nodes.

Overview

The CentralityRadarExporter generates a self-contained HTML page with an interactive radar chart and sortable ranking table. It computes all three centrality metrics using NodeCentralityAnalyzer and presents them in a visual, comparative format.

Features

  • Radar Chart: Spider/radar visualization of degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality
  • Multi-Node Comparison: Select up to 8 nodes with distinct colors
  • Sortable Table: Click column headers to sort by any metric
  • Top-N Filter: Focus on top 5, 10, 20, or all nodes
  • Search: Filter nodes by ID in real-time
  • Dark/Light Theme: Toggle between themes
  • JSON Export: Download selected or all centrality data
  • Summary Stats: At-a-glance most central nodes and averages

Usage

Java API

Graph<String, edge> graph = /* your JUNG graph */;
CentralityRadarExporter exporter = new CentralityRadarExporter(graph);
exporter.setTitle("Student Network Centrality");
exporter.export(new File("centrality-radar.html"));

// Or get HTML as string
String html = exporter.exportToString();

How It Works

  1. Computes degree, betweenness, and closeness centrality for every node
  2. Renders a ranking table sorted by combined score (0.3×degree + 0.4×betweenness + 0.3×closeness)
  3. Click any row to add that node to the radar chart (up to 8 simultaneous)
  4. The radar chart draws each node's three centrality values as a filled polygon
  5. Overlapping polygons reveal complementary strengths — e.g., a node with high betweenness but low degree

Interpreting the Chart

AxisMetricWhat It Means
Degree Normalized degree centrality How connected a node is (# connections / max possible)
Betweenness Betweenness centrality How often a node sits on shortest paths between others (bridge/broker role)
Closeness Closeness centrality How quickly a node can reach all others (1/avg distance)

Balanced profiles (even triangles) indicate well-rounded central nodes. Spiky profiles reveal specialists — e.g., a broker node with high betweenness but low degree.

Tips

  • Compare the top 3 nodes to see if the "most important" node depends on which metric you prioritize
  • Use the search box to find specific nodes in large graphs
  • Export JSON to feed centrality data into other tools or notebooks
  • The generated HTML is fully self-contained — share it without dependencies

GraphVisual Documentation

Balanced profiles (even triangles) indicate well-rounded central nodes. Spiky profiles reveal specialists — e.g., a broker node with high betweenness but low degree.

Tips

  • Compare the top 3 nodes to see if the "most important" node depends on which metric you prioritize
  • Use the search box to find specific nodes in large graphs
  • Export JSON to feed centrality data into other tools or notebooks
  • The generated HTML is fully self-contained — share it without dependencies

GraphVisual Documentation
